title: Two names, two different things
content: People often say 'ChatGPT' when they mean the model, and 'GPT' when they mean the app. But they are different things. GPT is a model - the engine. ChatGPT is an application built around that engine. The same split appears across many AI products, and understanding it makes the landscape much clearer.
next: An engine and a car - that analogy carries surprisingly far.

title: The engine and the car
content: A car engine is powerful but useless on its own - you cannot sit on it or steer it. The car adds seats, a wheel, pedals, and a dashboard to make that power usable. An LLM is the engine: raw capability. The chat app is the car: everything that makes the capability usable by a normal person.

title: Company, then model, then app
content: The chain has three links. OpenAI (the company) builds GPT-5 (the model), and ships ChatGPT (the app). Anthropic (the company) builds Claude Opus (the model), and ships the Claude app (the product). Google builds Gemini models and ships the Gemini app. Same pattern, every time.
next: What would it even be like to use the raw model?

title: The raw model is just text in, text out
content: A raw LLM has no chat bubbles, no history, no login. You send it text, it returns text - once. It does not remember your previous message unless that message is sent again. It cannot open a file or speak out loud. Everything you associate with 'chatting with AI' beyond pure text completion comes from somewhere else.
next: That somewhere else is the app layer - and it adds a lot.

title: What the app adds
content: The app wraps the model with other features: chat history, memory, file uploads, voice interfaces, tools such as web search or code execution, and a user interface. None of these features is the raw model itself, but all of them shape the user experience and strongly affect results.
next: This split explains a complaint you have probably heard.

title: 'Did the AI get worse?'
content: When people say a chatbot suddenly got better or worse, the model may not have changed at all - the surrounding app may have changed instead. A new system prompt, different memory behavior, or a changed tool can alter the experience even if the core model stays the same. Knowing the layers helps you diagnose where the change probably happened.
next: And one engine rarely powers just one car.

title: Same engine, many cars
content: The same Claude model powers the Claude chat app, the Claude Code programming tool, and thousands of third-party products built through an API. Same engine, different vehicles: a sedan, a truck, a race car. When you see an unfamiliar AI product, ask: which model is under the hood?
next: The names on those engines follow a pattern too.

title: Families and versions
content: Model names work like car model years. GPT-4, GPT-5: same family, newer generations. Claude Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku: one family with large, mid-size, and small variants - more capable versus faster and cheaper. Once you read names this way, AI headlines stop being alphabet soup.
next: But the biggest influence on your results is not the app or even the model.

title: The layer you control
content: You do not choose how the model was trained, and you barely control the app. But you fully control one layer: what you type. The same model with a sharper prompt can produce dramatically better results. That layer - prompting - is where your leverage lives.
next: Next: how to actually talk to these things.
